content: A coil 10 turns and a resistance of $$40 \Omega$$ is connected in series with B.G. of resistance $$30 \Omega$$. The coil is placed with its plane perpendicular to the direction of a uniform magnetic field of induction $$10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$$. If it is now turned through an angle of $$60^{\circ}$$ about on axis in its plane. Find the charge indicted in the coil. (Area of a coil $$=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^2$$ )
explanation:
Given, $$n=10$$ turns

$$R_{\text {coil }}=40 \Omega, R_G=-30 \Omega$$

total resistance in the circuit $$=40+30=70 \Omega$$

$$A=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^2, B=10^{-2} \mathrm{~T} \text {, and } \theta_1=0, \theta=60^{\circ}$$

we know that, flux of magnetic field is $$\phi$$

$$\phi=n B A$$

By Faraday's law, $$e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \quad(\because e=$$ induced emf$$)$$

$$\begin{aligned} i= & \frac{e}{R} \Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t}=\frac{d \phi}{R d t} \Rightarrow q=\frac{d \phi}{R} \\ q & =\frac{\phi_1-\phi_2}{R}=\frac{B n A \cos \theta_1-B n A \cos \theta_2}{R} \\ & =\frac{B n A\left(\cos 0^{\circ}-\cos 60^{\circ}\right)}{R} \\ q & =\frac{B n A(1-0.5)}{R}=\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{70} \\ & =\frac{50 \times 10^{-5}}{70} \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$q=0.714 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{C}$$. (Charge induced in a coil)

content: A disc of $$10.0 \mathrm{~cm}$$ diameter is rotated at speed of $$3600 \mathrm{~rpm}$$ inside a long solenoid of 1200 turns per metre on the axis of the solenoid and perpendicular to the plane of the disc. When a current of $$1.5 \mathrm{~A}$$ is passed through the solenoid, the difference in the potential between axes and circumference of the disc is nearly
explanation:
Radius of the disc,

$$r=\frac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~cm}=0.05 \mathrm{~m}$$

Number of rotations/s $$=\frac{3600}{60}=60$$

Number of turns per unit length of solenoid,

$$\begin{aligned} & n=1200 ~\text {turns} / \mathrm{m} \\ \therefore \quad & B=\mu_0 n l=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1200 \times 1.5 \mathrm{~T} \end{aligned}$$

The induced emf,

$$E=\frac{1}{2} B \omega r^2=1.08 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}=1.08 \mathrm{~mV}$$

content: The induced emf in a coil of $$10 \mathrm{H}$$ inductance in which current varies from $$9 \mathrm{~A}$$ to $$4 \mathrm{~A}$$ in $$0.2 \mathrm{~s}$$ is
explanation:
Included emf $$e=-L \frac{d i}{d t}$$

Given,

$$\begin{aligned} L & =10 \mathrm{H}, \Delta \mathrm{i}=9-4=5 \mathrm{~A}, d t=0.2 \mathrm{~s} \\ e & =10 \times \frac{5}{0.2}=250 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

content: A conductor of length $$5 \mathrm{~cm}$$ is moved parallel to itself with a speed of $$2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$, perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of $$10^{-3} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^3$$. The induced e.m.f. generated is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } e=-B v l \\ & \text { Given, } l=5 \mathrm{~cm}=5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}, v=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \text {, } \\ & B=10^{-3} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ & e=10^{-3} \times 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \\ & e=10 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~V} \\ & e=1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~V} \\ \end{aligned}$$
